confirmed.title: audit_logs created with ON DELETE SET NULL — immutability trigger added 31 days later, leaving a window where audit rows could be silently mutated
confirmed.code_evidence: ALTER TABLE "audit_logs" ADD CONSTRAINT "audit_logs_actorId_fkey" FOREIGN KEY ("actorId") REFERENCES "users"("id") ON DELETE SET NULL ON UPDATE CASCADE;
confirmed.description: At creation time (20260424), audit_logs.actorId FK is declared ON DELETE SET NULL. Any user deletion between migration 20260424111457 and the immutability trigger migration 20260525190000 (31 days later) silently issues an UPDATE on audit_logs rows, setting actorId to NULL. This mutates the audit record without triggering any protection. The schema comment and 20260525190000 migration both acknowledge that SET NULL on an immutable table is problematic — the FK was later changed to NO ACTION — but the 31-day window is unprotected. If any user was hard-deleted during that window in production, those audit rows were silently modified and the actor identity is permanently lost.
confirmed.root_cause: The immutability trigger and the FK ON DELETE behavior were designed together but deployed in two separate migrations 31 days apart, leaving the audit table mutable in the interim.
confirmed.impact: Any user deletion between 2026-04-24 and 2026-05-25 that had associated audit_logs rows permanently erased the actor identity in those rows; audit trail is no longer reliable for that period. Compliance and forensic audits may be affected.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Verify prod audit_logs for rows where actorId IS NULL and actorEmail (if added) is also NULL — these may be the result of the SET NULL window. Additionally, the original migration should have set ON DELETE RESTRICT or NO ACTION to protect immutability from the start. Document the window in a SECURITY_NOTE.
acceptance_criteria: No audit_logs row has actorId=NULL unless the user was deleted AFTER 2026-05-25 (when actorEmail snapshot was also captured)
Future audit_logs FK additions always use ON DELETE NO ACTION or RESTRICT
confirmed.verification_command: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM audit_logs WHERE "actorId" IS NULL AND "createdAt" < '2026-05-25';
confirmed.notes: Code evidence VERBATIM confirmed at line 24. The immutability trigger migration 20260525190000 lines 95-104 explicitly states 'A SetNull on user deletion issues an UPDATE on audit_logs that the immutability trigger (step 6) would reject', confirming this was a known design flaw fixed retroactively. Current schema is now protected (FK changed to NO ACTION), but historical impact is real and unrecoverable without a backup from the window period.

confirmed.title: time_entries.thirdPartyId ON DELETE CASCADE silently destroys billing/payroll records when a ThirdParty is deleted
confirmed.description: Time entries are billing and payroll records. When a ThirdParty row is deleted (soft-delete via isActive=false exists but hard-delete is not blocked), all associated time_entries rows are CASCADE deleted. This destroys historical billing/HR records without warning or audit trail. The sister constraint for user-linked time entries (time_entries.userId) is also ON DELETE CASCADE in schema.prisma (line 462), compounding the risk. For an HR management system in French local government, these records may be subject to retention obligations (up to 5 years under French labor law). There is no RESTRICT guard preventing ThirdParty deletion when time entries exist.
confirmed.root_cause: The FK was set to ON DELETE CASCADE following the same pattern as task_third_party_assignees (where CASCADE is correct for a join table), but time_entries are substantive financial/HR records that should survive their actor's deletion.
confirmed.impact: Deleting a third_party (intentionally or accidentally) irrecoverably destroys all associated time entries. These represent worked hours and may be legally required for payroll and billing purposes. No recovery path exists without a database backup.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Change ON DELETE CASCADE to ON DELETE RESTRICT on time_entries.thirdPartyId FK. If deletion of a ThirdParty with associated time entries must be allowed, implement a soft-delete pattern (ThirdParty.isActive already exists) and block hard-deletes via application logic or a BEFORE DELETE trigger that raises an exception when time_entries referencing that ThirdParty exist.
acceptance_criteria: DELETE FROM third_parties WHERE id = '...' fails with FK violation when time_entries reference that third_party
Integration test: create third_party + time_entry, attempt delete of third_party, expect error
confirmed.verification_command: SELECT tp.id, COUNT(te.id) as te_count FROM third_parties tp JOIN time_entries te ON te."thirdPartyId" = tp.id GROUP BY tp.id HAVING COUNT(te.id) > 0 LIMIT 5;
confirmed.notes: Code evidence VERBATIM confirmed at lines 159-161. Schema.prisma line 463 confirms ON DELETE Cascade. The hardDelete service (third-parties.service.ts line 180-187) explicitly comments 'Cascade FK handles time_entries, task_third_party_assignees, project_third_party_members' — meaning this CASCADE is intentional design, not an oversight. However, the finding remains valid: the design choice to allow hard-delete of a ThirdParty with associated time entries is architecturally dangerous for an HR/billing system with legal retention requirements. The application intentionally offers this endpoint, making this a design-level risk rather than an implementation bug.

confirmed.title: project_third_party_members.allocation INTEGER has no CHECK constraint for 0-100 range — unlike project_members.allocation which was fixed in DAT-004
confirmed.code_evidence:
"allocation"    INTEGER,
  "assignedById"  TEXT NOT NULL,
  "createdAt"     TIMESTAMP(3) NOT NULL DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
  CONSTRAINT "project_third_party_members_pkey" PRIMARY KEY ("id")
confirmed.description: project_third_party_members.allocation stores a percentage (0-100%) just like project_members.allocation. The DAT-003/DAT-004 business invariants migration (20260527120000) added CHECK ("allocation" BETWEEN 0 AND 100) for project_members but explicitly omitted project_third_party_members. This asymmetry means a buggy service path or direct SQL admin can insert allocation=-5 or allocation=150 for third-party members without any DB-level rejection. The schema comment for project_members reads '0-100%', implying the same semantic for both tables.
confirmed.root_cause: The DAT-004 migration added allocation range checks only for project_members but missed the parallel table project_third_party_members which has the same column with the same semantic.
confirmed.impact: Out-of-range allocation values for third-party members can corrupt workload calculations and capacity reports, leading to incorrect analytics. Applications may silently accept and display nonsensical values (e.g., -10% or 200% allocation).
confirmed.suggested_fix: Add: ALTER TABLE "project_third_party_members" ADD CONSTRAINT "project_third_party_members_allocation_ck" CHECK ("allocation" BETWEEN 0 AND 100);
acceptance_criteria: INSERT INTO project_third_party_members (..., allocation) VALUES (..., 101) fails with check_violation
INSERT INTO project_third_party_members (..., allocation) VALUES (..., -1) fails with check_violation
INSERT INTO project_third_party_members (..., allocation) VALUES (..., 50) succeeds
confirmed.verification_command: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM project_third_party_members WHERE allocation < 0 OR allocation > 100;
confirmed.notes: Code evidence VERBATIM confirmed at line 109 of the migration. Exhaustive search across all migrations confirms: project_members gets allocation CHECK in 20260527120000 (line 66: 'project_members_allocation_ck'), but project_third_party_members gets NO CHECK anywhere in the entire migration history. Current schema gap — not a historical window issue.

confirmed.title: predefined_task_recurring_rules.monthlyOrdinal and monthlyDayOfMonth have no range CHECK constraints despite documented semantics (1..5 and 1..31)
confirmed.code_evidence:
ALTER TABLE "predefined_task_recurring_rules" ADD COLUMN     "monthlyDayOfMonth" INTEGER,
ADD COLUMN     "monthlyOrdinal" INTEGER,
confirmed.description: The schema.prisma documents monthlyOrdinal as '1..5 (5 = dernière occurrence du mois) pour MONTHLY_ORDINAL' and monthlyDayOfMonth as '1..31 pour MONTHLY_DAY'. Neither column has a DB-level CHECK constraint anywhere in the migration history. An invalid monthlyDayOfMonth=32 or monthlyOrdinal=0 would be silently accepted, leading to recurrence rule evaluation errors at runtime (no matching day in month, undefined ordinal week). The business invariants migration (20260527120000) that fixed similar issues on weight, progress, allocation, etc. does not cover these fields.
confirmed.root_cause: The recurrence rule columns were added as raw INTEGER without accompanying CHECK constraints, and the subsequent DAT-003/DAT-004 cleanup missed them.
confirmed.impact: Invalid recurrence rules produce unpredictable task assignment behavior: monthlyDayOfMonth=32 generates no assignments or crashes the recurrence engine, monthlyOrdinal=6 may loop indefinitely or skip an entire month's assignments, corrupting the predefined task scheduling for HR teams.
confirmed.suggested_fix: ALTER TABLE "predefined_task_recurring_rules" ADD CONSTRAINT "ptrr_monthly_ordinal_ck" CHECK ("monthlyOrdinal" IS NULL OR ("monthlyOrdinal" BETWEEN 1 AND 5)); ALTER TABLE "predefined_task_recurring_rules" ADD CONSTRAINT "ptrr_monthly_day_of_month_ck" CHECK ("monthlyDayOfMonth" IS NULL OR ("monthlyDayOfMonth" BETWEEN 1 AND 31));
acceptance_criteria: INSERT with monthlyOrdinal=6 fails with check_violation
INSERT with monthlyDayOfMonth=32 fails with check_violation
confirmed.verification_command: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM predefined_task_recurring_rules WHERE ("monthlyOrdinal" IS NOT NULL AND "monthlyOrdinal" NOT BETWEEN 1 AND 5) OR ("monthlyDayOfMonth" IS NOT NULL AND "monthlyDayOfMonth" NOT BETWEEN 1 AND 31);
confirmed.notes: Code evidence VERBATIM confirmed at lines 8-9. Exhaustive grep across all migrations confirms no CHECK constraint exists for these two columns anywhere. Current schema gap — not a historical window issue.

confirmed.title: RBAC V4 drops role_permissions, permissions, role_configs tables and users.role column with no backup or preflight verification
confirmed.description: This migration irreversibly drops three tables (role_permissions, permissions, role_configs) and the users.role enum column, with only a comment advising to 'ensure a backup before prod deployment'. There is no preflight DO $$ ... RAISE EXCEPTION $$ block verifying that the V0 backfill was 100% complete (every user has a non-null roleId) before the legacy data is destroyed. Unlike the preceding V0 migration which has explicit post-backfill verification, V4 has none. If run on a database where V0's backfill had silently failed (e.g., a user with an unmapped legacy role left roleId NULL), the DROP destroys the only evidence of what roles existed.
confirmed.root_cause: The migration relies on a comment warning ('ensure backup') and the upstream V0 verification, but adds no self-contained guard to confirm the RBAC migration state before executing destructive DDL.
confirmed.impact: If V0's check was bypassed or the DB was in an intermediate state (partial backup restore, manual migration replay), V4 permanently destroys RBAC data for all users. In normal sequential Prisma migration deployment V0 must pass before V4 can run — but on partial DB restores or manual replay this ordering is not enforced.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Add a preflight DO block before the DROPs: DO $$ DECLARE null_count INTEGER; BEGIN SELECT COUNT(*) INTO null_count FROM users WHERE "roleId" IS NULL; IF null_count > 0 THEN RAISE EXCEPTION 'RBAC V4 preflight: % users still have NULL roleId — abort DROP', null_count; END IF; END $$;
acceptance_criteria: If any user has roleId IS NULL, the V4 migration fails with an informative exception
The migration succeeds only when all users have a valid roleId from V0 backfill
confirmed.verification_command: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM users WHERE "roleId" IS NULL;
confirmed.notes: Code evidence VERBATIM confirmed at lines 1-19 (the entire file). V0 migration (20260419192835) does have correct post-backfill verification (lines 100-107: RAISE EXCEPTION if any user has NULL roleId). In normal sequential Prisma deployment V0 must succeed before V4 runs. Risk is limited to partial DB restore or manual migration replay scenarios. Downgraded from high to medium confidence because the normal deployment path is protected by V0's guard.

confirmed.title: predefined_task_recurring_rules lacks a XOR constraint ensuring exactly one of dayOfWeek (WEEKLY) or monthly fields (MONTHLY_DAY/MONTHLY_ORDINAL) is populated per recurrenceType
confirmed.code_evidence:
ALTER TABLE "predefined_task_recurring_rules" ADD COLUMN     "monthlyDayOfMonth" INTEGER,
ADD COLUMN     "monthlyOrdinal" INTEGER,
ADD COLUMN     "recurrenceType" TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT 'WEEKLY',
    ALTER COLUMN "dayOfWeek" DROP NOT NULL;
confirmed.description: After this migration, dayOfWeek becomes nullable and two new monthly fields are added. The schema comment says dayOfWeek is NULL for MONTHLY_DAY recurrenceType. But no CHECK constraint enforces the cross-column invariant: when recurrenceType='WEEKLY', dayOfWeek must be non-null and monthly fields must be null; when recurrenceType='MONTHLY_DAY', monthlyDayOfMonth must be non-null; when recurrenceType='MONTHLY_ORDINAL', monthlyOrdinal must be non-null. Without this constraint, it is possible to store recurrenceType='WEEKLY' with dayOfWeek=NULL (null WEEKLY rule), or recurrenceType='MONTHLY_DAY' with both monthlyDayOfMonth and monthlyOrdinal populated (ambiguous rule). No later migration adds this cross-column guard.
confirmed.root_cause: The migration drops NOT NULL on dayOfWeek and adds the monthly alternatives without a compensating CHECK constraint to ensure exactly the right fields are populated per recurrenceType.
confirmed.impact: Invalid recurrence rule combinations silently accepted at the DB layer produce incorrect or absent task assignments, causing HR teams to miss mandatory weekly predefined tasks or generate duplicate monthly assignments. Bugs are hard to diagnose because the data looks syntactically valid.
confirmed.suggested_fix: ALTER TABLE "predefined_task_recurring_rules" ADD CONSTRAINT "ptrr_recurrence_fields_xor_ck" CHECK ( ("recurrenceType" = 'WEEKLY' AND "dayOfWeek" IS NOT NULL AND "monthlyDayOfMonth" IS NULL AND "monthlyOrdinal" IS NULL) OR ("recurrenceType" = 'MONTHLY_DAY' AND "monthlyDayOfMonth" IS NOT NULL AND "dayOfWeek" IS NULL AND "monthlyOrdinal" IS NULL) OR ("recurrenceType" = 'MONTHLY_ORDINAL' AND "monthlyOrdinal" IS NOT NULL AND "dayOfWeek" IS NULL AND "monthlyDayOfMonth" IS NULL) );
acceptance_criteria: recurrenceType='WEEKLY' with dayOfWeek=NULL is rejected by check_violation
recurrenceType='MONTHLY_DAY' with both monthlyDayOfMonth and monthlyOrdinal populated is rejected
recurrenceType='WEEKLY' with dayOfWeek=1 and NULL monthly fields succeeds
confirmed.verification_command: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM predefined_task_recurring_rules WHERE ("recurrenceType" = 'WEEKLY' AND "dayOfWeek" IS NULL) OR ("recurrenceType" = 'MONTHLY_DAY' AND "monthlyDayOfMonth" IS NULL) OR ("recurrenceType" = 'MONTHLY_ORDINAL' AND "monthlyOrdinal" IS NULL);
confirmed.notes: Code evidence VERBATIM confirmed at lines 8-11. The recurrenceType column was promoted to a proper enum by DAT-012 (20260527130000), but no XOR cross-column CHECK was added in DAT-012 or any other migration. The XOR pattern exists for time_entries actor fields in the same migration batch (lines 168-174: time_entries_actor_xor_check), confirming awareness of this pattern. Current schema gap.

refuted.reason: Historical window finding, impact fully remediated. DAT-013 (20260527140000) added CHECK constraints for predefined_tasks.startTime and endTime format. Critically, DAT-013 ran successfully in production — if any pre-existing invalid values (e.g., '25:99', 'morning') had existed from the 52-day window, DAT-013's ADD CONSTRAINT would have failed with a check_violation. The successful application of DAT-013 is itself proof that no malformed data was inserted during the window. The current schema is protected. The finding describes a real design gap but its impact hypothesis is contradicted by the successful migration deployment.

Historical window finding, impact fully remediated. DAT-012 (20260527130000) promoted completionStatus and recurrenceType from TEXT to proper PostgreSQL enums using USING (completionStatus::AssignmentCompletionStatus). DAT-012 ran successfully — if any invalid TEXT values (e.g., 'done' lowercase, 'DONE ' with trailing space) had existed, the USING cast would have failed with an invalid_text_representation error. Successful DAT-012 application proves no invalid values exist. The finding notes itself says 'DAT-012 already applied in production as of the audit date, so the conversion has likely succeeded. This is a design observation about the gap rather than a current production risk.' Refuted as a current risk.

Historical window finding, impact fully remediated. DAT-036 (20260528130000) added CREATE UNIQUE INDEX on clients.name and dropped the non-unique index. DAT-036 ran successfully — if duplicate client names had been inserted during the 35-day window, CREATE UNIQUE INDEX would have failed with a unique_violation error. Successful DAT-036 application proves no duplicate names exist in production. The finding notes itself states 'DAT-036 (20260528130000) has already been applied. This is a historical gap. Current production state should be clean post-DAT-036'. Refuted as a current risk.

Historical window finding, impact fully remediated. DAT-003 (20260527120000) added CHECK (endDate >= startDate) for school_vacations. DAT-003 ran successfully — if any row with endDate < startDate had been inserted during the 48-day window, ADD CONSTRAINT would have failed with a check_violation. Successful DAT-003 application proves no inverted date ranges exist. The finding notes itself states 'DAT-003 (20260527120000) confirmed to add school_vacations_dates_ck. This is a historical window issue, not a current schema gap.' Refuted as a current risk.
